_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-09 04:12:01Z)**_

I've explored the project. Here's a quick orientation before I scope the revision.

## About the Project

**Ubongo** (Swahili for "brain") is a personal, mood-aware AI assistant that lives in **Telegram**, designed for a single user (you). It's deliberately small: local-first, runs on a laptop or Pi, no cloud infra.

**Core idea**: a router (not an orchestrator) that picks a **persona** (Architect / Operator / Casual), a **skill** (only `summarize-conversation` in v0.1), and a **model** (via OpenRouter through LiteLLM) for each incoming message. State lives in SQLite, with a Markdown projection into an Obsidian-style vault. All outbound traffic flows through a notification queue with quiet-hours / holds.

**Stack (v0.1)**: Python 3.11+, python-telegram-bot, LiteLLM, OpenRouter, SQLite, YAML+Markdown configs. Explicitly **not** in scope: FastAPI, Redis, Docker, LangGraph, Temporal, Ollama, multi-agent orchestration.

**Build is staged across 7 phases** (skeleton → Telegram echo → LLM → tone classifier → SQLite memory → vault → skills → notification queue). v0.1 has 19 acceptance criteria and a hard cap of <3,000 LOC of Python (excluding tests).

## What the Documentation Looks Like Today

Four files, with real problems:

| File | Size | Status |
|---|---|---|
| [README.md](README.md) | 6.5 KB | Describes v0.1 reality — usable, minor inconsistencies |
| [Ubongo.md](Ubongo.md) | 13.8 KB | Conversation summary — early **vision** (multi-agent, LangGraph, debate mode) |
| [Ubongo_PRD.md](Ubongo_PRD.md) | 13.8 KB | **Bit-for-bit identical to `Ubongo.md`** — not actually a PRD |
| [UBONGO_BUILD.md](UBONGO_BUILD.md) | 45 KB | Detailed v0.1 build spec — the real source of truth |

**The big issues**: (1) `Ubongo.md` and `Ubongo_PRD.md` are duplicates; (2) the vision doc and the build spec describe scope-mismatched products (orchestrator vs. router); (3) no `CLAUDE.md` exists though `UBONGO_BUILD.md` references it; (4) zero code yet — it's 100% spec.

Before I write the plan, two scoping questions:

- **Targeted fixes only** - Resolve the duplicate file, fix obvious inconsistencies (model names, broken references), but leave file names and structure as-is.

**How should the multi-agent / orchestrator content in Ubongo.md (which conflicts with v0.1's router-based scope) be handled?**

```text
1	# Ubongo
2	
3	A personal, mood-aware AI assistant that lives in Telegram. One user. Adapts persona and model to the kind of conversation you're having. Capabilities are organized as composable skills with progressive disclosure. Outbound messages flow through a notification queue with a policy engine that respects quiet hours and ad-hoc holds.
4	
5	The name is Swahili for *brain* or *mind*.
6	
7	## Status
8	
9	v0.1 in development. See `UBONGO_BUILD.md` for the full build specification and phased plan.
10	
11	## What Ubongo Is
12	
13	A single-user Telegram bot. You talk to it; it talks back. Internally it picks one of three personas (Architect, Operator, Casual) based on the tone and intent of your message, runs the appropriate model via OpenRouter, and stores the conversation in SQLite with an Obsidian-compatible Markdown projection.
14	
15	## What Ubongo Is Not
16	
17	Not a multi-agent platform. Not a control plane for AI employees. Not a SaaS product. Not multi-channel. Not autonomous. Not self-improving.
18	
19	If you need orchestration of many agents toward business goals, look at Paperclip. If you need a coding agent toolkit, look at Pi or OpenClaw. Ubongo is narrower than all of them on purpose.
20	
21	## Tech Stack
22	
23	- Python 3.11+
24	- python-telegram-bot v21+
25	- LiteLLM (model routing)
26	- OpenRouter (model provider)
27	- SQLite (storage)
28	- YAML + Markdown (configuration)
29	- uv (package management)
30	
31	No FastAPI, Redis, Docker, or distributed anything. Single process, single user, single channel.
32	
33	## Prerequisites
34	
35	- Python 3.11 or newer
36	- [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) installed
37	- A Telegram bot token from [@BotFather](https://t.me/BotFather)
38	- An [OpenRouter](https://openrouter.ai/) API key
39	- Your Telegram numeric user ID (message [@userinfobot](https://t.me/userinfobot) on Telegram to get it)
40	
41	## Setup
42	
43	```bash
44	git clone <repo-url> ubongo
45	cd ubongo
46	uv sync
47	```
48	
49	Copy the environment template and fill in your secrets:
50	
51	```bash
52	cp .env.example .env
53	```
54	
55	Edit `.env`:
56	
57	```
58	OPENROUTER_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
59	TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123456:ABC-...
60	```
61	
62	Edit `config/settings.yaml` and set `telegram.allowed_user_ids` to your Telegram numeric ID:
63	
64	```yaml
65	telegram:
66	  allowed_user_ids: [YOUR_NUMERIC_ID]
67	```
68	
69	Optionally edit `config/UBONGO.md` to customize the global identity context. This file is loaded for every conversation regardless of persona, so it's where you put facts about yourself and communication preferences.
70	
71	Optionally edit `config/personas/*.md` to tune voices.
72	
73	## Run
74	
75	```bash
76	uv run python -m ubongo
77	```
78	
79	The bot starts polling Telegram. Send it a message from your allowed account; it should respond.
80	
81	If you get no response, check:
82	
83	1. The bot is actually running (`Ubongo starting` should appear in the logs).
84	2. Your numeric Telegram ID matches `allowed_user_ids` exactly. Wrong ID means silent drop.
85	3. `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` is set in `.env` and the file is loaded.
86	
87	## Usage
88	
89	Send any text message. Ubongo will classify the intent and tone, pick a persona, and respond.
90	
91	Slash commands:
92	
93	- `/architect`, `/operator`, `/casual` — force a persona for the current session
94	- `/auto` — return to automatic persona selection
95	- `/summary` — summarize the current conversation
96	- `/skills` — list available skills
97	- `/reload` — reload personas, skills, and global context without restarting
98	- `/hold 3h` — hold non-urgent notifications for 3 hours
99	- `/hold until 18:00` — hold until 6pm today
100	- `/hold` — hold until you say `/resume`
101	- `/resume` — release any active hold and deliver queued items
102	- `/queue` — show what's currently held
103	- `/quiet` — show or change quiet hours
104	
105	Quiet hours and holds only affect *proactive* messages (which arrive once the scheduler ships in v0.3). Synchronous responses to your messages always come through, even at 3am.
106	
107	## Configuration
108	
109	| File | Purpose |
110	|---|---|
111	| `.env` | Secrets only. Never committed. |
112	| `config/settings.yaml` | Models, memory tuning, delivery policy, logging. |
113	| `config/UBONGO.md` | Global identity and communication preferences. Loaded for every persona. |
114	| `config/personas/*.md` | Voice-specific overlays for each persona. |
115	| `config/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` | Skill definitions. Frontmatter + body. |
116	| `config/routing.yaml` | Tone/intent to persona/skill mapping rules. |
117	| `config/urgency.yaml` | Urgency assignment rules (used in v0.3). |
118	
119	Edit any of these and run `/reload` in Telegram to apply without restart (except `settings.yaml`, which requires a restart).
120	
121	## Vault
122	
123	Daily conversation logs are written to `vault/daily/YYYY-MM-DD.md` in Obsidian-compatible Markdown. Open the `vault/` directory as an Obsidian vault to browse them. The vault is gitignored by default; if you want versioned history of your conversation logs, run `git init` inside `vault/` separately.
124	
125	In v0.1 the vault is write-only. The system reads from SQLite, never from the Markdown files. Bidirectional sync is on the v0.2 list.
126	
127	## Project Structure
128	
129	```
130	ubongo/
131	  config/             # all user-editable configuration
132	    UBONGO.md         # global identity context
133	    settings.yaml
134	    routing.yaml
135	    personas/
136	    skills/
137	  src/ubongo/
138	    bot.py            # Telegram handlers
139	    classifier.py     # tone/intent classification
140	    router.py         # persona and skill selection
141	    context.py        # hierarchical prompt assembly
142	    events.py         # named event dispatcher
143	    llm.py            # LiteLLM wrapper
144	    skills.py         # skill registry with progressive disclosure
145	    personas.py
146	    delivery/         # notification queue and policy engine
147	    memory/           # SQLite store, compaction, vault projection
148	  vault/              # generated daily notes (gitignored)
149	  tests/
150	```
151	
152	See `UBONGO_BUILD.md` for the full architecture and the phased build plan.
153	
154	## Roadmap
155	
156	**v0.1 (current):** Telegram, three personas, tone routing, SQLite memory, Markdown vault, skills infrastructure with one demo skill, notification queue with quiet hours and holds.
157	
158	**v0.2:** Pick at most two from: CLI front-end, Google Calendar integration, embedding-based recall, topic-aware compaction, structured fact extraction, bidirectional vault sync, a fourth persona.
159	
160	**v0.3:** Scheduler for proactive jobs (cron-style). Additional integrations as skills (email, news, Reddit). Each integration is a CLI script the agent invokes via a single `bash` tool, not a first-class tool definition.
161	
162	The roadmap is loose on purpose. Build v0.1, use it for two weeks, prioritize v0.2 from observed friction rather than from architectural ambition.
163	
164	## License
165	
166	TBD. Personal project; not currently published.
167
```
</details></tool-use>

2	
3	Overview
4	
5	This document summarizes the conceptual architecture, design discussions, and technical direction for Ubongo, a personal experimental adaptive multi-agent assistant inspired by OpenClaw, Hermes, orchestrated cognitive runtimes, and persistent memory systems.
6	
7	The discussion evolved from analyzing projects such as OpenClaw and Hermes into defining a lightweight, modular, orchestrated personal AI ecosystem capable of:
8	
9	* Multi-agent orchestration
10	* Dynamic LLM routing
11	* Long-term memory
12	* Personality adaptation
13	* Workflow automation
14	* Self-healing execution
15	* Controlled runtime evolution
16	* Human governance and approval systems
17	* Parallel agent execution
18	* Wiki-first memory architecture
19	* Experimental Genetic Programming optimization
20	
21	The system is envisioned less as a chatbot and more as:
22	
23	* A personal adaptive AI mind
24	* A modular cognitive playground
25	* A persistent digital companion
26	* A mood-aware orchestration runtime
27	
28	⸻
29	
30	Initial Inspiration
31	
32	OpenClaw

35	
36	* Persistent assistants
37	* Tool orchestration
38	* Multi-channel interaction
39	* Local/self-hosted deployments
40	* Agent workflows
41	* Memory systems
42	* MCP integration

45	
46	* Large ecosystem
47	* Integration flexibility
48	* Multi-tool orchestration
49	* Strong community momentum
50	
51	Weaknesses identified:
52	
53	* Infrastructure-heavy
54	* Complex orchestration
55	* Security risks around autonomy
56	* Limited governance structure
57	
58	⸻
59	
60	Hermes Agent
61	
62	Hermes was discussed as a more memory-centric and persistent personal assistant.
63	
64	Strengths identified:
65	
66	* Long-term memory orientation
67	* Self-improving workflows
68	* Lightweight deployment
69	* Persistent user context
70	
71	OpenClaw and Hermes together inspired the idea of a broader orchestration runtime.
72	
73	⸻
74	
75	Core Architectural Direction
76	
77	Main Insight
78	
79	The future system should not be:
80	
81	* One chatbot
82	* One model
83	* One personality
84	* One monolithic agent
85	
86	Instead, it should behave like:
87	
88	* A distributed cognitive runtime
89	* A digital organization of agents
90	* A governed orchestration platform

93	
94	Master Agent Concept
95	
96	The central architectural idea introduced was the concept of a Master Agent.
97	
98	Responsibilities
99	
100	The Master Agent acts similarly to:
101	
102	* Engineering Manager
103	* Workflow orchestrator
104	* Cognitive scheduler
105	* Governance system
106	
107	Responsibilities include:
108	
109	* Task decomposition
110	* Agent creation and destruction
111	* Workflow coordination
112	* Risk evaluation
113	* Personality routing
114	* Conflict resolution
115	* Approval management
116	* Context management
117	* Model routing coordination
118	
119	The Master Agent should:
120	
121	* Spawn specialized agents dynamically
122	* Monitor agent execution
123	* Stop or replace agents
124	* Trigger retries and repairs
125	* Coordinate memory usage
126	* Evaluate user intent and tone
127	
128	⸻
129	
130	Specialized Worker Agents
131	
132	The system should include specialized worker agents.
133	
134	Examples discussed:
135	
136	Agent Responsibility
137	Research Agent Search, retrieval, synthesis
138	Coding Agent Code generation and refactoring
139	Evaluator Agent Validate outputs and correctness
140	Repair Agent Recover failed workflows
141	Memory Agent Manage memory consolidation

144	Persona Agents Different communication styles

147

150	Durable intelligence should live in:
151	
152	* Memory
153	* Workflows
154	* Policies
155	* Governance
156	* Knowledge systems
157	
158	not in individual agents.

164	Initial Position
165	
166	Initially, the idea was proposed that users should interact only with a stable user-facing persona.

168	Reasons:
169	
170	* Maintain continuity
171	* Reduce chaos
172	* Avoid conflicting personalities
173	* Preserve memory consistency
174	* Simplify governance

176	⸻

181	
182	Users should be able to interact directly with worker agents and specialized personas intentionally.

184	Examples:
185	
186	Persona Purpose
187	Professional Work interactions
188	Architect Technical discussions
189	Critic Brutal analysis
190	Casual Friendly interactions
191	Adult/Private Informal mature interactions
192	Operator Fast execution mode
193	Researcher Long-form analysis

195	Key insight:
196	
197	Different personalities should share:

199	* Same memory core
200	* Same governance layer
201	* Same orchestration runtime
202	
203	but expose different behavioral layers.

207	Tone-Aware Routing

209	The system should adapt dynamically to:
210	
211	* User tone
212	* Context
213	* Intent
214	* Risk
215	* Historical preferences
216	
217	Examples:

220	Formal Professional persona
221	Technical Architect/coding agents
222	Playful Casual or critic persona

224	Adult/private Mature/private persona
225	
226	Key principle:

229	
230	Risk influences governance.

235	
236	A central realization was that autonomy must be governed.
237	
238	The Manager Agent should decide when to:
239	
240	* Proceed automatically
241	* Ask for clarification
242	* Request approval
243	* Escalate to the user

249	
250	Examples:
251	
252	* Summaries may execute automatically
253	* Sending emails may require approval
254	* Destructive actions always require confirmation
255	
256	⸻
257	
258	Multi-Model Routing
259	
260	A core requirement identified was the ability to use multiple LLMs simultaneously.
261	
262	Examples:
263	
264	Task Model Type
265	Intent classification Small fast model
266	Summarization Cheap model
267	Coding Strong coding model
268	Planning High reasoning model
269	Conversational interaction Personality-focused model
270	Retrieval ranking Embedding/reranker model

272	The system should dynamically select models based on:

274	* Cost
275	* Latency
276	* Capability
277	* Context size
278	* Privacy
279	* Reliability

281	LiteLLM was proposed as the routing abstraction layer.

285	Memory Architecture
286	
287	Initial Memory Model
288	
289	Layered memory was proposed:
290	
291	Memory Type Purpose
292	Short-term Active context
293	Working Current workflows/goals
294	Episodic Historical interactions
295	Semantic Stable facts
296	Procedural Learned workflows

302	Wiki-First Memory Architecture
303	
304	A major improvement discussed was using an Obsidian-compatible Markdown wiki as the canonical memory layer.
305	
306	Key insight:
307	
308	The vector database should not be the source of truth.
309	
310	The Markdown vault should be the durable human-readable memory.
311	
312	Architecture:
313	
314	Markdown Wiki (Canonical Memory)
315	        ↓
316	Git Versioning
317	        ↓
318	Embeddings Index
319	        ↓
320	Graph Relationships
321	
322	Benefits:
323	
324	* Human-readable memory
325	* Editable by users
326	* Git versioning
327	* Obsidian compatibility
328	* Graph relationships
329	* Long-term maintainability

336	  /projects

338	  /workflows
339	  /preferences
340	  /daily
341	  /decisions
342	  /system
343

347	
348	Memory updates should pass through a dedicated Memory Agent.
349

351	
352	Parallel and Multithreaded Agent Execution
353	
354	The discussion evolved into adding support for parallel agent execution.
355	
356	Execution modes discussed:
357	
358	Mode Description
359	Sequential One after another
360	Parallel Independent concurrent execution
361	Competitive Multiple agents solve same task
362	Collaborative Agents work on different subtasks
363	Debate Agents argue opposing views
364	Speculative Cheap agents start before stronger validation
365	
366	Benefits:
367	
368	* Faster execution
369	* Better answer quality
370	* Redundant validation
371	* Lower latency
372	* Better exploration

374	Risks:

377	Race conditions Shared-state locking
378	Conflicting outputs Evaluator aggregation
379	Memory corruption Single-writer memory model

381	Infinite spawning Lifecycle limits
382	
383	Critical design principle:
384	
385	Agents may run in parallel, but durable state mutation must be controlled.
386

390	Long-term memory Memory Agent
391	Workflow state Workflow runtime
392	User-facing response Persona/Response Composer
393	Runtime code changes Runtime evolution pipeline

406	* Rollbacks
407	* Workflow repair

420	Controlled Runtime Evolution
421	
422	A major topic discussed was autonomous runtime improvement.
423	
424	Important distinction:
425	
426	The system should not rewrite itself freely in production.
427	
428	Instead:
429	
430	Observe issue

432	Propose change
433	  ↓
434	Implement in sandbox

436	Run tests

438	Evaluator review

444	Allowed autonomous changes initially:

446	* Prompt optimization

448	* Tool routing improvements
449	* Retry strategy improvements

461	
462	The possibility of using Genetic Programming (GP) was explored.
463	
464	Conclusion:
465	
466	GP can provide value as an optimization engine, not as the primary intelligence layer.
467	
468	Good GP targets:
469	
470	Optimization Target Suitability
471	Prompt variants Excellent
472	Routing policies Excellent
473	Tool chains Good
474	Retry strategies Good
475	Workflow sequences Good
476	Runtime core mutation Dangerous
477	
478	Key concept:
479	
480	GP requires measurable fitness functions.
481	
482	Example:
483	
484	Fitness = SuccessRate
485	        - Cost
486	        - Latency
487	        - HallucinationRate
488	        - UserCorrections
489	
490	The GP system should operate like:
491	
492	Generate variants
493	  ↓
494	Sandbox evaluation
495	  ↓
496	Measure fitness
497	  ↓
498	Promote successful candidates
499	  ↓
500	Require approval for critical changes
501

# Documentation Revision — Ubongo

## Context

Ubongo is a personal, mood-aware Telegram AI assistant for a single user. Today it's 100% specification, 0% code. Four documentation files exist, with several structural problems:

1. **`Ubongo.md` and `Ubongo_PRD.md` are bit-for-bit identical** (both 13,813 bytes) — neither is actually a PRD; both are the same conceptual conversation summary.
2. **Scope mismatch**: `Ubongo.md` describes an enterprise-grade orchestrator (Master Agent, LangGraph, Temporal, debate mode, Genetic Programming). `UBONGO_BUILD.md` deliberately scopes v0.1 down to a single-user router-based bot. A reader hitting `Ubongo.md` first will misunderstand the project.
3. **`CLAUDE.md` is referenced inside `UBONGO_BUILD.md` but doesn't exist** in the repo — Claude Code sessions have no project-level context file to load.
4. **README sections (Setup, Run, Usage) read as if the code already exists** — but `src/`, `pyproject.toml`, and configs don't. New readers will be confused.
5. **No status / progress tracker** — phases 0–7 are defined, but there's no record of which are done.

User wants a **full restructure** that preserves the vision content as origin/inspiration.

## Files Affected

| Path | Action |
|---|---|
| [README.md](README.md) | Edit — clarify pre-code status, fix link refs |
| [Ubongo.md](Ubongo.md) | Rename → `UBONGO_VISION.md`, prepend disclaimer |
| [Ubongo_PRD.md](Ubongo_PRD.md) | Delete (exact duplicate) |
| [UBONGO_BUILD.md](UBONGO_BUILD.md) | Edit — update internal references to renamed Vision doc; verify nothing else points at deleted PRD |
| `CLAUDE.md` | Create — derived from `UBONGO_BUILD.md`'s "CLAUDE.md (for future Claude Code sessions)" section |
| `STATUS.md` | Create — current implementation state, phase tracker |

## Per-File Changes

### 1. Delete `Ubongo_PRD.md`

It is a byte-for-byte duplicate of `Ubongo.md` (verified via file sizes; created 3 minutes apart). No content is lost by removal. Use `rm`, not git rm (repo is not a git repo per environment context).

### 2. Rename `Ubongo.md` → `UBONGO_VISION.md` and prepend disclaimer

The content is valuable as the design's origin story but is **not the v0.1 spec**. Prepend a top-of-file block:

> It captures the broad architectural exploration — Master Agent orchestration, parallel agents, governance layers, Genetic Programming — that shaped the project's intent. The actual v0.1 build deliberately scopes most of this *out*: it's a single-user Telegram bot with a stateless router (no Master Agent), three personas, and no agent lifecycle.

\```

Leave the body unchanged — it's a faithful record of the design conversation and shouldn't be retconned.

### 3. Edit `README.md`

Targeted edits, not a rewrite. The README is mostly good; it just needs to stop reading as "the code works today."

- **Status section (line 7–9)**: replace "v0.1 in development" with something explicit: "v0.1 specification complete; implementation has not started. The `Setup`, `Run`, and `Usage` sections below describe the *intended* behavior of the v0.1 build. See [STATUS.md](STATUS.md) for current phase progress."
- **Setup section (line 41–72)**: prepend a one-line note: "These instructions describe the v0.1 target. They will not work until Phase 0 of [UBONGO_BUILD.md](UBONGO_BUILD.md) is complete."

  ## Documentation Map
  - [README.md](README.md) — this file. Goal, setup, usage, roadmap.
  - [UBONGO_BUILD.md](UBONGO_BUILD.md) — full v0.1 build specification, phased plan, acceptance criteria. The source of truth for what we're building.
  - [UBONGO_VISION.md](UBONGO_VISION.md) — origin/inspiration document. Conceptual exploration that shaped Ubongo. Not the v0.1 spec.
  - [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) — context for Claude Code sessions working on this repo.

  \```
- **License section**: leave as-is.

### 4. Edit `UBONGO_BUILD.md`

Minimal touch. Search/replace any references to `Ubongo.md` or `Ubongo_PRD.md` and replace with `UBONGO_VISION.md`. Verify nothing else points at the deleted PRD. No content changes beyond link integrity.

### 5. Create `CLAUDE.md`

Recommend handling at Phase 0 implementation, not now.)

- Converting `UBONGO_VISION.md` into a formal PRD with user stories. User chose to preserve as vision; not turning it into a PRD.

4. Reading `README.md` top-to-bottom as a new contributor should answer: *what is this, is the code ready, where do I look next.* The current README answers #1 and #3 but is ambiguous on #2.
5. Reading `UBONGO_VISION.md` should make it immediately clear (in the first paragraph) that the document is **historical/aspirational**, not the v0.1 spec.
6. `CLAUDE.md` exists and a fresh Claude Code session opening this repo would have enough context to know what's built, what isn't, and where the spec lives.

No tests to run — this is documentation only.

<tool-use data-tool-type="generic" data-tool-name="AskUserQuestion"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **AskUserQuestion**</summary>
**What CLI form do you want for v0.1?**

Options:
- **Interactive REPL (Recommended)** - `uv run python -m ubongo` opens a chat loop. You type, it replies, persona/skill routing runs each turn. Closest analogue to the Telegram experience and preserves the conversational session model that the memory/compaction logic was designed around.
- **One-shot commands** - `ubongo send "..."` for each message; state persists to SQLite between calls. Simpler implementation, scriptable, but every invocation cold-loads context. No long-lived 'session' until you build one on top.
- **Both — REPL primary, one-shot for scripting** - REPL is the daily driver; `ubongo send` exists for cron jobs, scripts, and the future scheduler. More surface area to maintain but matches how the project will eventually be used.

**How should Telegram be treated in the docs going forward?**

Options:
- **Defer to v0.2, keep design intact (Recommended)** - v0.1 is CLI-only. Telegram moves to v0.2 as a planned channel with the existing design (allowed_user_ids, queue, quiet hours, holds) preserved for later. README/build spec/vision still mention Telegram as the eventual second channel.
- **Remove from v0.1 docs entirely; revisit later** - Strip all Telegram-specific sections (slash commands, queue/quiet-hours/holds, allowed_user_ids) from v0.1 documentation. Re-introduce them only when you're actually ready to build that channel. Cleaner v0.1 spec but loses the queued-design context.
- **Keep mentioned, but mark explicitly as 'future channel'** - Telegram stays referenced throughout the docs as the motivating use case and target channel, but every Telegram-specific section is wrapped with a 'v0.2+, not built in v0.1' marker. Heaviest read but preserves all design intent.

**What about the notification queue + quiet-hours/holds policy engine, which were designed for proactive Telegram delivery?**

Options:
- **Defer with Telegram to v0.2 (Recommended)** - Quiet hours and holds only matter when there's an out-of-band channel that pages you. In CLI, every response is synchronous to your prompt. Move the queue + policy engine out of v0.1 entirely; resurrect when Telegram lands.
- **Keep a minimal queue in v0.1** - Implement a SQLite-backed outbound queue and `/queue` CLI command, but skip quiet-hours/holds policy until Telegram exists. Useful if v0.1 will eventually feed scheduled jobs that print queued messages on next CLI invocation.
- **Keep full queue + policy in v0.1 anyway** - Build the full queue and policy engine even though CLI doesn't need it, so the abstraction is in place when Telegram lands. Costs LOC budget against the <3,000 cap and adds testing burden for code that has no v0.1 user.


**Answer:** Both — REPL primary, one-shot for scripting, Remove from v0.1 docs entirely; revisit later, Keep a minimal queue in v0.1
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	# Ubongo - Build Specification
2	
3	This document is the build spec for Ubongo. It is structured as phased releases with explicit acceptance criteria. v0.1 ships a useful, working system. v0.2 and v0.3 are sketched so that v0.1 makes structural choices that don't have to be undone later, but they are not part of the v0.1 build.
4	
5	Build v0.1 first. Use it for two weeks. Then reassess what's actually missing before starting v0.2.
6	
7	## What Ubongo Is
8	
9	A personal, mood-aware AI assistant that lives in Telegram. One user (Giuseppe). Runs locally or on a small box (laptop, Pi 5). Adapts persona and model to the kind of conversation you're having. Capabilities are organized as composable skills with progressive disclosure. Outbound messages flow through a notification queue with a policy engine that respects quiet hours and ad-hoc holds. Extension points are named events so v0.2+ behavior plugs in without rewriting the core loop.
10	
11	## What Ubongo Is Not (v0.1)
12	
13	- A multi-agent orchestration platform
14	- A multi-channel system (no Slack, no WhatsApp, no Discord, no web UI)
15	- A self-improving runtime
16	- A production system
17	- A SaaS product
18	
19	If a feature in the original PRD is not explicitly listed below, it is out of scope for v0.1. This includes: Master Agent, worker agents (Research/Coding/Evaluator/Repair/Execution), parallel agent execution, debate mode, speculative execution, Genetic Programming, runtime self-modification, prompt evolution, sandboxing, embedding-based memory recall, semantic search, observability dashboards, and approval gates beyond text confirmation.
20	
21	## Core Design Decisions
22	
23	1. **Single channel: Telegram.** Bot API is first-class, no daemon, no linked device. CLI may be added in v0.2.
24	2. **Router, not orchestrator.** A function that picks a persona, optionally one skill, and a model. No agent lifecycle, no spawning, no shutdown.
25	3. **Personas are configuration.** Markdown files containing system prompts. Personas define *voice*. Identity boilerplate is in a single global file (`UBONGO.md`), not duplicated across personas.
26	4. **Skills are configuration with progressive disclosure.** Folders with a `SKILL.md` and supporting prompts. Skills define *capabilities*. Only descriptions are loaded at startup; bodies load on activation. Personas and skills compose: any persona can run any skill.
27	5. **Hierarchical context loading.** System prompt for any turn is built by concatenating, in order: global context (`UBONGO.md`), active persona (`personas/<name>.md`), active skill body (if any). Closer-to-task layers come last.
28	6. **Memory: SQLite canonical, Markdown projected.** Conversation logs and structured facts live in SQLite. A daily notes Markdown file is generated for human inspection in Obsidian. Vault is write-only in v0.1.
29	7. **Compaction is a swappable function.** v0.1 ships a default (last N turns verbatim, prior history collapsed into a summary). The seam exists so v0.2 can swap in topic-aware or persona-specific strategies without touching memory storage.
30	8. **Models via OpenRouter.** Single API key, every model behind one interface, routed through LiteLLM. No Ollama in v0.1.
31	9. **All outbound messages flow through a notification queue.** Even synchronous responses to user messages technically pass through the queue, though they're delivered immediately at `urgent` priority. Proactive messages (when the scheduler exists) use the same path with no special-casing.
32	10. **Policy engine governs delivery.** Quiet hours, ad-hoc holds, hold-until-ack, and per-message urgency are first-class concepts.
33	11. **Extension points are named events.** The bot's main loop emits events at well-defined points (`before_classify`, `after_classify`, `before_recall`, `after_recall`, `before_llm`, `after_llm`, `before_send`, `after_send`). v0.1 has only default handlers; v0.2+ adds handlers without modifying the core loop.
34	12. **Tool discipline.** v0.1 exposes zero tools to the LLM. When tools are added (v0.2+), prefer CLI scripts invoked via a single `bash` tool over first-class tool definitions. New first-class tools require justification.
35	13. **Configuration in YAML, secrets in `.env`.** Code reads config; config never contains secrets; secrets are loaded from environment at startup.
36	14. **One user.** Hardcode the allowed Telegram user ID in config. Reject everything else.
37	
38	## Tech Stack
39	
40	| Layer | Technology | Why |
41	|---|---|---|
42	| Language | Python 3.11+ | Ecosystem, LiteLLM, fast iteration |
43	| Bot | python-telegram-bot (v21+) | Mature, async, well-documented |
44	| LLM routing | LiteLLM | Provider abstraction without lock-in |
45	| Model provider | OpenRouter | Single key, every model, easy A/B |
46	| Storage | SQLite (stdlib) | Single-user, zero-ops, sufficient |
47	| Config | YAML + Markdown | Editable without touching code |
48	| Secrets | python-dotenv | Standard `.env` loading |
49	| Tests | pytest | Standard |
50	| Package management | uv | Fast, modern |
51	
52	No FastAPI, no Redis, no Qdrant, no LangGraph, no Docker for v0.1. If you find yourself reaching for them, stop and justify.
53	
54	## Architecture
55	
56	```
57	Telegram message
58	    |
59	    v
60	auth check (allowlist)
61	    |
62	    v   [event: before_classify]
63	classifier (small model via OpenRouter, JSON output)
64	    |   -> {intent, tone, task_type, skill?, confidence}
65	    v   [event: after_classify]
66	router (deterministic mapping)
67	    |   -> (persona, skill?, model)
68	    v   [event: before_recall]
69	memory recall (SQLite, last N turns + compacted older history)
70	    |   [event: after_recall]
71	    v
72	prompt assembly (UBONGO.md + persona.md + skill.md? + history)
73	    |   [event: before_llm]
74	    v
75	LLM call (LiteLLM -> OpenRouter -> chosen model)
76	    |   [event: after_llm]
77	    v
78	enqueue response in notification queue (urgency=urgent)
79	    |
80	    v   [event: before_send] (policy engine runs as a handler here)
81	delivery worker checks policy, sends to Telegram
82	    |   [event: after_send]
83	    v
84	memory write (SQLite + Markdown daily note)
85	```
86	
87	For proactive messages (v0.3+), the flow starts at "enqueue" and skips the user-message path. Same delivery worker, same policy engine, same `before_send` handlers.
88	
89	## File Structure
90	
91	```
92	ubongo/
93	  pyproject.toml
94	  README.md
95	  CLAUDE.md                    # context for future Claude Code sessions
96	  .env.example
97	  .gitignore                   # includes /vault, *.db, .env
98	
99	  config/
100	    UBONGO.md                  # global identity and instructions (hierarchical root)
101	    settings.yaml              # general config
102	    routing.yaml               # tone/intent -> persona/skill rules
103	    urgency.yaml               # urgency assignment rules (used in v0.3)
104	    personas/
105	      architect.md             # voice-specific overlay only
106	      operator.md
107	      casual.md
108	    skills/
109	      summarize-conversation/
110	        SKILL.md
111	        prompts/
112	          summarize.md
113	
114	  src/ubongo/
115	    __init__.py
116	    __main__.py                # entry point: python -m ubongo
117	    bot.py                     # Telegram handlers, main loop, event dispatch
118	    events.py                  # named event registry and dispatcher
119	    classifier.py              # tone/intent/skill classification
120	    router.py                  # persona + skill + model selection
121	    llm.py                     # LiteLLM wrapper
122	    context.py                 # hierarchical context loader (UBONGO.md + persona + skill)
123	    personas.py                # persona loading
124	    skills.py                  # skill registry: descriptions at startup, bodies on demand
125	    config.py                  # config loading, env var resolution
126	    delivery/
127	      __init__.py
128	      queue.py                 # notification queue (enqueue, dequeue)
129	      policy.py                # delivery policy engine (a before_send handler)
130	      worker.py                # background delivery loop
131	      catchup.py               # catch-up summarizer
132	      commands.py              # /hold, /resume, /quiet, /queue
133	    memory/
134	      __init__.py
135	      schema.sql
136	      store.py                 # SQLite operations
137	      compaction.py            # swappable compaction function (default impl in v0.1)
138	      vault.py                 # Markdown daily notes projection
139	
140	  tests/
141	    test_classifier.py
142	    test_router.py
143	    test_skills.py
144	    test_context.py
145	    test_events.py
146	    test_memory.py
147	    test_compaction.py
148	    test_delivery_queue.py
149	    test_delivery_policy.py
150	
151	  vault/                       # gitignored, generated daily notes
152	```
153	
154	## Personas (v0.1)
155	
156	Three personas. More can come later. Each lives in `config/personas/<name>.md` and contains *only* voice-specific instructions. Identity boilerplate (who Giuseppe is, communication preferences, formatting rules) lives in `config/UBONGO.md` and is loaded for every persona.
157	
158	**Architect.** Deep technical reasoning. Used for system design, RFCs, code architecture, technical tradeoffs. Calm, structured, willing to push back. Heavy model.
159	
160	**Operator.** Fast execution. Used for "do X" requests, status checks, quick lookups. Terse, direct, action-oriented. Heavy model but with low max_tokens.
161	
162	**Casual.** Friendly conversation. Used for low-stakes chat, brainstorming, venting, end-of-day decompression. Warm, less structured. Lighter model is fine.
163	
164	Example `config/UBONGO.md` (the global file, loaded for every turn):
165	
166	```markdown
167	# Ubongo Global Context
168	
169	You are Ubongo, Giuseppe Turitto's personal AI assistant.
170	
171	## About Giuseppe
172	- Senior Engineering Manager at Kiwi.com, based in Madrid.
173	- Originally Venezuelan, lived in NYC, now in Madrid.
174	- 20+ years in software, 10+ in engineering leadership.
175	- Working on a leadership book ("Still Human") and several side projects.
176	
177	## Communication Defaults
178	- Direct. Skip hedging and filler.
179	- Assume strong technical and management foundations.
180	- Push back when you see gaps in reasoning.
181	- Default to prose over bullet points unless he asks for a list.
182	- No em-dashes.
183	- No emojis unless he uses them first.
184	- Minimal markdown in conversation.
185	
186	## Memory
187	You have access to memory of past conversations and a structured fact store.
188	Use it. Do not narrate retrieval.
189	```
190	
191	Example `config/personas/architect.md` (voice overlay only, no identity boilerplate):
192	
193	```markdown
194	---
195	name: architect
196	default_model: openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.5
197	max_tokens: 4096
198	---
199	
200	You are in Architect mode. Help Giuseppe with deep technical reasoning:
201	system design, architecture decisions, code structure, tradeoff analysis.
202	
203	In this mode:
204	- Take time to think before answering complex design questions.
205	- Make tradeoffs explicit; never present a recommendation without naming what
206	  it costs.
207	- When the user proposes an architecture, identify at least one assumption
208	  worth challenging before agreeing.
209	- Use prose paragraphs. Reserve lists for genuinely list-shaped content.
210	```
211	
212	The system prompt for an Architect-mode turn is `UBONGO.md` body + `architect.md` body, concatenated. If a skill is active, its body is appended after.
213	
214	## Skills with Progressive Disclosure
215	
216	Skills are reusable capabilities, separate from persona voice. Any persona can invoke any skill. A skill is a folder under `config/skills/` with this shape:
217	
218	```
219	config/skills/<skill-name>/
220	  SKILL.md             # frontmatter (always loaded) + body (loaded on activation)
221	  prompts/             # optional, named prompt templates
222	    <name>.md
223	  references/          # optional, static reference material
224	    <name>.md
225	```
226	
227	`SKILL.md` frontmatter:
228	
229	```yaml
230	---
231	name: summarize-conversation
232	description: Produce a brief summary of the current conversation session. Activates on /summary or when the user asks to summarize what's been discussed.
233	trigger:
234	  commands: ["/summary"]
235	  intents: []
236	default_urgency: normal       # used when this skill produces proactive output (v0.3+)
237	default_persona: operator     # optional preferred persona; router can override
238	---
239	```
240	
241	**Progressive disclosure.** At startup, the skill registry indexes each skill by name, description, and trigger metadata only. The body of `SKILL.md` is *not* loaded into memory. When the router selects a skill (by command match, intent match in the classifier output, or `/skill <name>`), the body is read from disk and appended to the system prompt for that turn only.
242	
243	This matters because:
244	
245	- The classifier sees the full list of skill *descriptions* and can include a `skill` field in its JSON output, naming a relevant skill from a possibly large library.
246	- The system prompt for each turn only includes the body of skills that are actually being used, not the entire skill catalog.
247	- Token cost scales with active skills, not registered skills. You can have 50 skills loaded without paying tokens for 49 of them on every turn.
248	
249	**Skill resolution order:**
250	
251	1. Explicit command (`/summary` -> `summarize-conversation`).
252	2. `skill` field in classifier JSON output (if non-null and matches a registered skill).
253	3. Manual selection via `/skill <name>` for the next message.
254	
255	A message can have zero or one skill active in v0.1. Multi-skill composition is v0.2+.
256	
257	**v0.1 ships exactly one skill: `summarize-conversation`.** The point in v0.1 is to validate the skills infrastructure and progressive disclosure, not to ship a library.
258	
259	## Hierarchical Context Loader
260	
261	Implemented in `context.py`. Single function `build_system_prompt(persona_name, skill_name=None) -> str` that:
262	
263	1. Reads `config/UBONGO.md` body.
264	2. Reads `config/personas/<persona_name>.md` body (skipping frontmatter).
265	3. If `skill_name` is provided, reads `config/skills/<skill_name>/SKILL.md` body (skipping frontmatter), prefixed with `## Active Skill: <name>`.
266	4. Concatenates with double newlines between sections.
267	5. Returns the assembled string.
268	
269	Caching: persona files and `UBONGO.md` are cached in memory at startup and on `/reload`. Skill bodies are read on demand and cached per skill name (cleared on `/reload`).
270	
271	This means changing `UBONGO.md` updates every persona's behavior on next `/reload` without editing three files. Adding a new persona means writing only the voice-specific overlay, not duplicating identity.
272	
273	## Named Events
274	
275	Implemented in `events.py`. Simple synchronous dispatcher:
276	
277	```python
278	class EventBus:
279	    def on(self, event: str, handler: Callable) -> None: ...
280	    def emit(self, event: str, payload: dict) -> dict: ...   # returns possibly-modified payload
281	```
282	
283	Handlers receive a payload dict and return a (possibly modified) payload. Multiple handlers per event run in registration order. Default handlers ship in v0.1; v0.2+ adds handlers without modifying the bot loop.
284	
285	**v0.1 events and their default handlers:**
286	
287	| Event | Payload | Default handler |
288	|---|---|---|
289	| `before_classify` | `{message, session}` | none (passthrough) |
290	| `after_classify` | `{message, session, classification}` | none (passthrough) |
291	| `before_recall` | `{session, max_turns}` | none (passthrough) |
292	| `after_recall` | `{session, history}` | compaction (if history > N) |
293	| `before_llm` | `{system_prompt, messages, model}` | none (passthrough) |
294	| `after_llm` | `{response, tokens_in, tokens_out, model}` | memory write |
295	| `before_send` | `{queue_item, now}` | policy engine (decides deliver/hold) |
296	| `after_send` | `{queue_item, telegram_message_id}` | vault projection |
297	
298	The bot loop calls `events.emit(...)` at each point and uses the returned payload. This is the seam for everything in v0.2+:
299	
300	- Embedding-based recall: `after_recall` handler that augments `history` with semantically relevant past messages.
301	- Fact extraction: `after_llm` handler that runs over the user message and proposes facts to store.
302	- Redaction: `before_send` handler that scrubs sensitive content.
303	- Audit logging: handlers on every event writing to a structured log.
304	
305	For v0.1, the only non-passthrough handlers are compaction (`after_recall`), memory write (`after_llm`), policy engine (`before_send`), and vault projection (`after_send`). All others are stubs that return the payload unchanged. The point of registering them in v0.1 is so the architecture is in place.
306	
307	## Tone Classifier with Skill Selection
308	
309	This is the part that has to actually work. v0.1 design:
310	
311	**Single LLM call** to a small model via OpenRouter (e.g., `openrouter/qwen/qwen-2.5-7b-instruct` or `openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.2-3b-instruct`). Prompt asks for structured JSON:
312	
313	```json
314	{
315	  "intent": "technical|work|casual|research|notification_control|other",
316	  "tone": "neutral|focused|frustrated|playful",
317	  "task_type": "question|discussion|command|venting",
318	  "skill": null,
319	  "confidence": 0.0
320	}
321	```
322	
323	The classifier prompt includes the list of available skill names and descriptions so the model can suggest one in the `skill` field, or `null` if none applies. This is how progressive disclosure connects to routing without an extra LLM call.
324	
325	`notification_control` as an intent: messages like "hold notifications for 2 hours" or "stop bothering me until 6pm" are routed to the delivery commands handler in Phase 7, not to a normal LLM response.
326	
327	**Routing rules** (`config/routing.yaml`):
328	
329	```yaml
330	rules:
331	  - match: { intent: notification_control }
332	    handler: delivery_command       # bypass normal LLM flow
333	  - match: { intent: technical }
334	    persona: architect
335	  - match: { intent: work, task_type: command }
336	    persona: operator
337	  - match: { intent: casual }
338	    persona: casual
339	  - match: { tone: frustrated }
340	    persona: casual
341	default:
342	  persona: architect
343	```
344	
345	**Hysteresis.** Within a single session (messages within 30 minutes of each other), the persona only switches if the new classification has confidence > 0.7 AND differs from the active persona. This prevents whiplash from a single offhand message.
346	
347	**Manual override.** Slash commands `/architect`, `/operator`, `/casual` force the persona for the current session. `/auto` returns to automatic routing.
348	
349	**Failure mode.** If classification fails or returns malformed JSON, fall back to the default persona with no skill. Log the failure. Never block on classifier failure.
350	
351	## Memory Model
352	
353	**SQLite as canonical store.** Schema:
354	
355	```sql
356	CREATE TABLE conversations (
357	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
358	  started_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
359	  ended_at TIMESTAMP,
360	  active_persona TEXT
361	);
362	
363	CREATE TABLE messages (
364	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
365	  conversation_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES conversations(id),
366	  role TEXT NOT NULL,           -- user | assistant | system
367	  content TEXT NOT NULL,
368	  timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
369	  persona TEXT,
370	  skill TEXT,
371	  model TEXT,
372	  tokens_in INTEGER,
373	  tokens_out INTEGER
374	);
375	
376	CREATE TABLE summaries (
377	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
378	  conversation_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES conversations(id),
379	  covers_from_message_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
380	  covers_to_message_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
381	  content TEXT NOT NULL,
382	  strategy TEXT NOT NULL,       -- name of the compaction function used
383	  created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL
384	);
385	
386	CREATE TABLE sessions (
387	  user_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
388	  last_message_at TIMESTAMP,
389	  active_persona TEXT,
390	  override_until TIMESTAMP,
391	  current_conversation_id INTEGER REFERENCES conversations(id)
392	);
393	
394	CREATE TABLE facts (
395	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
396	  subject TEXT NOT NULL,
397	  predicate TEXT NOT NULL,
398	  object TEXT NOT NULL,
399	  source_message_id INTEGER REFERENCES messages(id),
400	  created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
401	  importance INTEGER DEFAULT 0
402	);
403	-- facts table is created in v0.1 but population is v0.2+
404	
405	CREATE TABLE notification_queue (
406	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
407	  content TEXT NOT NULL,
408	  urgency TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (urgency IN ('low', 'normal', 'urgent')),
409	  source TEXT,                  -- 'response' | 'skill:<name>' | 'job:<name>'
410	  source_skill TEXT,
411	  created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
412	  deliver_after TIMESTAMP,
413	  delivered_at TIMESTAMP,
414	  expires_at TIMESTAMP,
415	  metadata TEXT                 -- JSON blob
416	);
417	
418	CREATE TABLE delivery_policy (
419	  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
420	  type TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (type IN ('hold', 'hold_until_ack', 'quiet_hours_override')),
421	  until TIMESTAMP,
422	  urgency_threshold TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (urgency_threshold IN ('low', 'normal', 'urgent')),
423	  created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
424	  source TEXT,
425	  active BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
426	);
427	
428	CREATE INDEX idx_messages_conversation ON messages(conversation_id);
429	CREATE INDEX idx_summaries_conversation ON summaries(conversation_id);
430	CREATE INDEX idx_queue_undelivered ON notification_queue(delivered_at) WHERE delivered_at IS NULL;
431	CREATE INDEX idx_policy_active ON delivery_policy(active) WHERE active = TRUE;
432	```
433	
434	**Recall and compaction.** `memory/compaction.py` defines a swappable function:
435	
436	```python
437	def compact(history: list[Message], target_turns: int) -> tuple[Optional[Summary], list[Message]]:
438	    """
439	    Given a full message history and a target number of recent turns to keep
440	    verbatim, return (summary_of_older, recent_turns).
441	    Default v0.1 implementation: simple recency split with single-paragraph summary
442	    of everything older than `target_turns`.
443	    """
444	```
445	
446	The default v0.1 implementation runs a small LLM call to summarize older messages into one paragraph. The summary is persisted in the `summaries` table so the same range isn't re-summarized on every turn. Recall returns: existing summary (if any) + the most recent N=10 messages.
447	
448	This is the seam for v0.2+ to swap in topic-aware compaction (split history by topic shift, summarize each topic separately) or persona-specific compaction (architect persona preserves code blocks verbatim, casual persona summarizes aggressively) without touching `store.py`.
449	
450	**Markdown projection.** A daily note at `vault/daily/YYYY-MM-DD.md` containing chronological conversation log with timestamps, persona, and content. Written by a default `after_send` handler.
451	
452	```markdown
453	# 2026-05-07
454	
455	## 09:14 [architect]
456	**You:** [message]
457	**Ubongo:** [response]
458	
459	## 11:32 [casual]
460	...
461	```
462	
463	The system reads from SQLite, never from Markdown. Bidirectional sync is v0.2+.
464	
465	## Notification Queue and Delivery Policy
466	
467	Every outbound Telegram message goes through the queue, even direct responses to user messages. This is structural, not optional. When v0.3 adds the scheduler, proactive messages use the same delivery path. If you start by sending some messages directly and others via the queue, you'll have to migrate every send site later. Build it once.
468	
469	The policy engine is a `before_send` event handler. v0.2+ can add additional `before_send` handlers (redaction, format adjustment, urgency boosting based on rules) without modifying the worker.
470	
471	**Synchronous responses** are enqueued with `urgency=urgent` and `deliver_after=NOW()`. The delivery worker picks them up immediately. To the user, this feels instant.
472	
473	**Proactive messages** (v0.3+) are enqueued with their declared urgency and an optional `deliver_after`. The worker decides when they go.
474	
475	**The policy engine.** Given a queued message and the current state, answers: "can this be delivered now?" using:
476	
477	1. The message's urgency.
478	2. Active overrides in the `delivery_policy` table.
479	3. Quiet hours from `config/settings.yaml`.
480	4. Current time in the configured timezone.
481	
482	A message is delivered iff its urgency meets or exceeds the highest currently active threshold.
483	
484	**Slash commands:**
485	
486	- `/hold 3h` — hold all non-urgent for 3 hours.
487	- `/hold until 18:00` — hold until 6pm today.
488	- `/hold` — `hold_until_ack`, no expiry.
489	- `/resume` — clear all active holds. Trigger catch-up summarization.
490	- `/queue` — list queued items by urgency.
491	- `/quiet` — show current quiet hours.
492	- `/quiet 22-08` — change quiet hours (runtime override).
493	- `/quiet off` — disable quiet hours.
494	
495	**Natural language detection.** When the classifier returns `intent: notification_control`, the message is routed to a small handler that interprets the instruction and applies it via the same code paths as the slash commands. The handler confirms in plain language. Ambiguous phrasing falls back to suggesting slash commands.
496	
497	**Catch-up on release.** When `/resume` runs (or a hold expires) and >= `summarize_threshold` items are pending, summarize them via an LLM call using the casual persona. Mark the underlying items delivered.
498	
499	**Hold-until-ack safety.** If a `hold_until_ack` has been active for more than `hold_until_ack_warning_hours` (default 24), the worker sends a single urgent ping asking whether to keep holding.
500	
501	**Worker loop.** Asyncio task in the same process as the bot. Wakes every `worker_poll_seconds` (default 30). Also wakes immediately when an item is enqueued at `urgent`.
502	
503	**Expiry.** Items with `expires_at` in the past are dropped without delivery and logged.
504	
505	## Tool Discipline
506	
507	v0.1 exposes zero tools to the LLM. The LLM produces text; the bot sends text. No tool calling, no function calling.
508	
509	When tools are added in v0.2+:
510	
511	1. **Default to CLI scripts.** A new capability is a CLI binary or Python script with a README. The agent (when given tool access) uses a single `bash` tool to invoke it. The README is read on demand.
512	2. **First-class tools require justification.** A new entry in the LLM's tool list must justify why it can't be a CLI script. Acceptable reasons include latency-critical paths, structured output the LLM needs to reason about across turns, or required side-effect isolation.
513	3. **Tool descriptions are tax.** Every tool definition costs tokens on every turn it's available. Audit the tool list quarterly; remove unused tools.
514	
515	This discipline is borrowed from Pi (the agent toolkit). The argument: progressive disclosure for capabilities, same as for skills. You'll have ten tools eventually if you're not careful, and most of them won't earn the context cost.
516	
517	## Configuration Files
518	
519	### `.env.example`
520	
521	```
522	# Required
523	OPENROUTER_API_KEY=
524	TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=
525	
526	# Optional, for future phases
527	GOOGLE_CALENDAR_CLIENT_ID=
528	GOOGLE_CALENDAR_CLIENT_SECRET=
529	GMAIL_CLIENT_ID=
530	GMAIL_CLIENT_SECRET=
531	REDDIT_CLIENT_ID=
532	REDDIT_CLIENT_SECRET=
533	```
534	
535	### `config/settings.yaml`
536	
537	```yaml
538	telegram:
539	  allowed_user_ids: [123456789]   # set to your Telegram numeric ID
540	
541	models:
542	  default: openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.5
543	  classifier: openrouter/qwen/qwen-2.5-7b-instruct
544	  casual: openrouter/anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5
545	  compaction: openrouter/anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5
546	
547	api_keys:
548	  openrouter:
549	    env: OPENROUTER_API_KEY
550	
551	memory:
552	  recall_turns: 10
553	  session_timeout_minutes: 30
554	  compaction:
555	    strategy: default              # name of registered compaction function
556	    trigger_at_turns: 30           # compact when total turns exceed this
557	
558	vault:
559	  path: ./vault
560	  daily_notes_subdir: daily
561	
562	delivery:
563	  quiet_hours:
564	    enabled: true
565	    start: "23:00"
566	    end: "07:00"
567	    timezone: "Europe/Madrid"
568	    urgency_threshold: urgent
569	  default_urgency_threshold: normal
570	  hold_until_ack_warning_hours: 24
571	  worker_poll_seconds: 30
572	  catchup:
573	    summarize: true
574	    summarize_persona: casual
575	    summarize_threshold: 2
576	
577	logging:
578	  level: INFO
579	  format: json
580	```
581	
582	### `config/routing.yaml`
583	
584	See "Tone Classifier" section above.
585

587	
588	```yaml
589	rules: []
590	```
591	
592	## CLAUDE.md (for future Claude Code sessions)
593	
594	The project ships with a `CLAUDE.md` at the root containing:
595	
596	- One-paragraph project description.
597	- "What Ubongo Is Not" (verbatim from this doc).
598	- Current phase status.
599	- Pointer to this build spec.
600	- Convention notes: prose over bullets, no em-dashes, no emojis, direct tone (Giuseppe's preferences, baked into the project).
601	- Architectural rules: every outbound message goes through the queue; secrets only in `.env`; new capabilities ship as skills, not as `bot.py` modifications; new behavior ships as event handlers, not as core-loop edits; new tools default to CLI scripts.
602	
603	## Phased Build Plan
604	
605	Each phase ships a working system. Don't move to phase N+1 until N is verified.
606	
607	### Phase 0: Project Skeleton and Hierarchical Context
608	
609	**Goal:** Working Python project with config loading, hierarchical context assembly, and structured logging.
610	
611	**Tasks:**
612	- Initialize project with `uv init` and `pyproject.toml`.
613	- Create the file structure above.
614	- Write `.env.example` with required vars.
615	- Write `config/settings.yaml`, `config/UBONGO.md`, and stubs for `routing.yaml` and `urgency.yaml`.
616	- Write README.md with setup instructions.
617	- Write CLAUDE.md with project context.
618	- Implement `config.py`: load YAML, resolve env vars referenced in config, validate required fields at startup.
619	- Implement `context.py`: hierarchical loader that concatenates `UBONGO.md` + persona file + optional skill body.
620	- Implement `events.py`: EventBus with `on(event, handler)` and `emit(event, payload) -> payload`.
621	- Set up structured JSON logging to stderr.
622	
623	**Acceptance:** `uv run python -m ubongo` starts, loads config, prints "Ubongo starting" with config summary (no secrets in the log), exits cleanly on Ctrl-C. Calling `context.build_system_prompt("architect")` returns the concatenation of `UBONGO.md` + `architect.md`. Registering a handler on a stub event and emitting it returns the modified payload.
624	
625	### Phase 1: Telegram Echo Bot with Persona Switching
626	
627	**Goal:** Bot responds to messages from the allowed user. Slash commands switch personas. Stub responses, no LLM yet.
628	
629	**Tasks:**
630	- Implement `bot.py` with python-telegram-bot.
631	- Reject any message from a non-allowed user (silent drop, log it).
632	- Handle text messages: echo back with current persona name.
633	- Handle `/architect`, `/operator`, `/casual`, `/auto`, `/start`, `/help`.
634	- Active persona stored in-process for now; SQLite in Phase 4.
635	
636	**Acceptance:** Send "hello" from your allowed account, get `[architect] hello`. Send `/casual`, send "hello", get `[casual] hello`. Send from another account, get nothing.
637	
638	### Phase 2: LLM Integration via OpenRouter with Hierarchical Prompts
639	
640	**Goal:** Real LLM responses through LiteLLM and OpenRouter. System prompts assembled via the hierarchical loader.
641	
642	**Tasks:**
643	- Implement `personas.py`: load persona files (frontmatter + body) at startup.
644	- Implement `llm.py`: thin wrapper around LiteLLM. Function `complete(system_prompt, messages, model, max_tokens) -> CompletionResult`.
645	- Wire bot to: build system prompt via `context.build_system_prompt(persona)`, call LLM, return response.
646	- Wire `before_llm` and `after_llm` events (passthrough handlers in v0.1).
647	- Handle LLM errors: retry once with exponential backoff, then send a polite error message.
648	
649	**Acceptance:** Send a technical question with `/architect`, get a real Architect-style response. Send a casual message with `/casual`, get a real Casual-style response. The personas feel different. Editing `UBONGO.md` and restarting changes behavior across all personas.
650	
651	### Phase 3: Tone Classifier and Auto Routing
652	
653	**Goal:** In `/auto` mode, the system classifies each message and picks the persona. Manual overrides still work.
654	
655	**Tasks:**
656	- Implement `classifier.py`: function takes message, returns structured result. Use LiteLLM with the classifier model. JSON output mode if supported; defensive parsing otherwise. Classifier prompt includes empty skill list for now (skills come in Phase 6).
657	- Implement `router.py`: load `config/routing.yaml`, apply rules, return persona. Apply hysteresis logic.
658	- Wire into bot via `before_classify` and `after_classify` events (passthrough handlers).
659	- Log classifier output and routing decision for every message.
660	
661	**Acceptance:** In `/auto` mode, "help me design a circuit breaker" routes to architect. "ugh today sucked" routes to casual. Five technical messages then one casual within a minute does not flip persona unless confidence is high.
662	
663	### Phase 4: SQLite Conversation Memory with Swappable Compaction
664	
665	**Goal:** Conversations persist across restarts. Recall returns recent turns plus a compaction summary for older history.
666	
667	**Tasks:**
668	- Implement `memory/schema.sql`. Run migrations on startup (simple `CREATE IF NOT EXISTS`).
669	- Implement `memory/store.py`: start/get/end conversations, append messages, get session state, get last N messages, persist summaries.
670	- Define "session": same user, time gap < 30 minutes since last message.
671	- Implement `memory/compaction.py`: register a default compaction function. Trigger when total session turns exceed `compaction.trigger_at_turns`. Persist resulting summary in the `summaries` table.
672	- Wire `after_recall` event with the compaction handler.
673	- Wire `after_llm` event with the memory write handler.
674	- Move active persona / override state from in-process dict to `sessions` table.
675	
676	**Acceptance:** Multi-turn conversation. Restart the bot. Continue; bot remembers last 10 turns plus a summary of older turns. Wait 31 minutes; next message starts a new session. Replace the default compaction function with a stub returning a fixed string; verify recall uses the stub.
677	
678	### Phase 5: Markdown Vault Projection
679	
680	**Goal:** Daily notes generated in Obsidian-compatible format, written by an `after_send` handler.
681	
682	**Tasks:**
683	- Implement `memory/vault.py`: function `append_to_daily_note(date, user_message, bot_response, persona)`.
684	- Register as default `after_send` handler.
685	- Verify rendering in Obsidian.
686	- README notes that user can `git init` inside `vault/` for projection history.
687	
688	**Acceptance:** After a day of use, `vault/daily/2026-MM-DD.md` exists with a clean log. Open in Obsidian; renders correctly. Disabling the handler stops vault writes; re-enabling resumes them.
689	
690	### Phase 6: Skills with Progressive Disclosure
691	
692	**Goal:** Skills can be defined as folders. Descriptions load at startup; bodies load on demand. Classifier can suggest a skill in its JSON output. v0.1 ships one skill.
693	
694	**Tasks:**
695	- Implement `skills.py`: discover skills in `config/skills/`, parse `SKILL.md` frontmatter only at startup. Body is loaded on demand and cached per skill.
696	- Extend `classifier.py`: include the list of registered skill names and descriptions in the classifier prompt; expect a `skill` field in JSON output.
697	- Extend `router.py`: when a skill is selected (by command, classifier output, or `/skill <name>`), load the body and pass it to the context builder. The context builder appends it as `## Active Skill: <name>` after the persona section.
698	- Implement the `summarize-conversation` skill: triggers on `/summary`, summarizes the current session in 3-5 sentences using the operator persona.
699	- Add `/skills` command listing available skills (names + descriptions).
700	- Add `/reload` command that re-reads `UBONGO.md`, personas, skill metadata, and clears the skill body cache.
701	
702	**Acceptance:** `/summary` produces a coherent summary of the current session. `/skills` lists `summarize-conversation`. Editing the skill's `SKILL.md` body and running `/reload` reflects the change without restart. Verifying that the skill body is *not* in the system prompt for messages where the skill isn't triggered (inspect the `before_llm` payload).
703	
704	### Phase 7: Notification Queue and Delivery Policy
705	
706	**Goal:** Every outbound message flows through the queue. Delivery is governed by quiet hours and ad-hoc holds. Slash commands manage holds. The policy engine is registered as a `before_send` handler.
707	
708	Build in sub-steps; test each.
709	
710	**Sub-step 7a: Queue and immediate delivery.**
711	- Implement `delivery/queue.py`: `enqueue(content, urgency, source, ...)`, `dequeue_deliverable() -> List[Item]`, `mark_delivered(id)`.
712	- Implement `delivery/worker.py`: asyncio task. Polls every `worker_poll_seconds`. Wakes immediately on `urgent` enqueue.
713	- Refactor existing bot response path: enqueue at urgency `urgent`, source `response`. Telegram send happens in the worker after `before_send` event passes.
714	
715	*Acceptance:* Bot still feels instant. All Telegram sends go through the queue (verifiable in DB).
716	
717	**Sub-step 7b: Policy engine as event handler.**
718	- Implement `delivery/policy.py`: function `effective_threshold(now) -> Urgency` consulting quiet hours and active `delivery_policy` rows. Function `can_deliver(item, now) -> bool`.
719	- Register `policy_check` as the default `before_send` handler. If it returns a payload with `deliver=False`, the worker leaves the item in the queue and updates `deliver_after`.
720	
721	*Acceptance:* Set quiet hours to current time. Send a message; response is enqueued at `normal`-equivalent (not urgent for synchronous responses; this needs handling — see note below) and held. Disable quiet hours; response delivers.
722	
723	*Note on synchronous responses and quiet hours:* synchronous responses to direct user messages are enqueued at `urgent` so they break through quiet hours. Otherwise the user would send a message during quiet hours and get no reply, which is broken. Only proactive messages (v0.3) get held by quiet hours. Document this clearly.
724	
725	**Sub-step 7c: Slash commands.**
726	- Implement `delivery/commands.py`: handlers for `/hold`, `/resume`, `/quiet`, `/queue`.
727	
728	*Acceptance:* `/hold 1m`, send a `low`-urgency test item via debug, verify it's held. After 1 minute, item delivers.
729	
730	**Sub-step 7d: Catch-up summarizer.**
731	- Implement `delivery/catchup.py`. On `/resume` or hold expiry with >= `summarize_threshold` pending items, generate one summary message via the casual persona.
732	
733	*Acceptance:* Hold for an hour, manually inject 3 fake queue items, `/resume`, get a single summary.
734	
735	**Sub-step 7e: Notification control via natural language.**
736	- Add `notification_control` intent to the classifier prompt.
737	- When detected, route to the delivery commands handler. Confirm in plain language. Fall back to slash command suggestions on ambiguity.
738	
739	*Acceptance:* "hold notifications for 2 hours" creates a 2-hour hold with confirmation. "stop bothering me until 6pm" creates a hold until 18:00.
740	
741	**Sub-step 7f: Hold-until-ack safety.**
742	- Worker checks for `hold_until_ack` policies older than `hold_until_ack_warning_hours`. Sends single urgent ping.
743	
744	*Acceptance:* `/hold`, advance the policy's `created_at` to 25 hours ago in SQLite, observe the worker sends the warning ping on its next cycle.
745	
746	## Acceptance Criteria for v0.1 Complete
747	
748	You are done when all of the following are true:
749	
750	1. The bot responds to your Telegram messages and ignores everyone else.
751	2. Manual `/architect`, `/operator`, `/casual` commands work and feel different.
752	3. In `/auto` mode, persona is selected automatically and feels mostly right.
753	4. You can correct auto-selection with a slash command.
754	5. `UBONGO.md` is loaded for every persona; editing it changes behavior across all personas after `/reload`.
755	6. Conversation context persists across bot restarts within a session.
756	7. New session starts after 30 minutes of inactivity.
757	8. Compaction kicks in past the configured threshold; older history is replaced by a summary in recall; the summary is persisted and not regenerated each turn.
758	9. Daily notes write to the Obsidian vault and render correctly.
759	10. The `summarize-conversation` skill works via `/summary`. Skill bodies are *not* loaded until activation (verifiable in logs or memory inspection).
760	11. `/reload` picks up edits to `UBONGO.md`, personas, and skill metadata without restart.
761	12. Every outbound message goes through the notification queue.
762	13. Quiet hours hold proactive (non-synchronous) messages of insufficient urgency. Synchronous responses break through.
763	14. `/hold`, `/resume`, `/quiet`, `/queue` all work as specified.
764	15. Natural-language hold instructions are recognized and confirmed.
765	16. Catch-up after release produces a single summary, not a flood.
766	17. Named events fire at all the documented points; default handlers do their work; registering a no-op handler doesn't break the flow.
767	18. The system survives a full day of real use without crashing.
768	19. Total project size is under 3000 lines of Python (excluding tests). Hierarchical context, events, and progressive disclosure justify the bump from 2500.
769	
770	If line 19 is failing, you've over-built. Cut.
771	
772	## Out of Scope (v0.1) - Explicit Reminder
773	
774	Deferred deliberately. Don't sneak them in.
775	
776	- Slack, WhatsApp, Discord, web UI, voice
777	- Master Agent, worker agents, agent lifecycle management
778	- Parallel agent execution, debate mode, competitive mode, speculative execution
779	- Genetic Programming, runtime self-modification, prompt evolution
780	- Embedding-based memory recall, semantic search
781	- Topic-aware or persona-specific compaction strategies (the seam exists; the implementations come later)
782	- Bidirectional Markdown sync (vault is write-only in v0.1)
783	- Approval gates beyond text confirmation
784	- Sandboxing, tool execution (zero tools in v0.1), MCP server integration
785	- Multi-user support, RBAC, team features
786	- Self-healing workflows, retry orchestration beyond single retries
787	- Observability dashboards, distributed tracing
788	- Docker, Kubernetes, FastAPI, Redis, Qdrant, Memgraph, Temporal, NATS
789	- Scheduler / cron-style jobs (v0.3)
790	- External integrations: calendar, email, Reddit, news (v0.2 onward, one at a time)
791	
792	---
793	
794	## v0.2 Sketch (Not Part of v0.1 Build)
795	
796	After two weeks of v0.1 use, prioritize from this list. Pick at most two.
797	
798	- **CLI front-end.** Same router, same memory, same queue, same events. Different transport.
799	- **First external integration: Google Calendar.** OAuth flow, token storage in SQLite, one skill `calendar-review` activating on `/calendar` or calendar-related questions. Still request-response; no scheduler yet. First real tool: a CLI script `ubongo-calendar` invoked via a `bash` tool exposed to the LLM.
800	- **Embedding-based recall.** Add `sqlite-vec`. Embed each message at write time. Register an `after_recall` handler that augments history with semantically relevant past messages. The default compaction handler stays in place.
801	- **Topic-aware compaction.** Replace the default compaction function with one that detects topic shifts and summarizes per-topic. Swap in via config.
802	- **Structured fact extraction.** Populate the `facts` table. An `after_llm` handler runs over user messages and proposes facts. Surfaced via an `after_recall` handler when relevant.
803	- **Bidirectional vault sync.** A file watcher on `vault/` detects edits and ingests them as proposed updates to SQLite, mediated by a confirmation prompt.
804	- **A fourth persona.** Critic, Researcher, or Private. Only add if you've felt the lack.
805	
806	## v0.3 Sketch (Not Part of v0.1 Build)
807	
808	The scheduler. Adds proactive behavior on top of v0.2's integrations.
809	
810	- **`config/jobs.yaml`** declares scheduled jobs:
811	
812	```yaml
813	jobs:
814	  - name: morning_calendar_brief
815	    schedule: "0 7 * * *"
816	    skill: calendar-review
817	    persona: operator
818	    delivery:
819	      urgency: normal
820	    enabled: true
821	
822	  - name: friday_inbox_triage
823	    schedule: "0 16 * * 5"
824	    skill: email-triage
825	    persona: architect
826	    delivery:
827	      urgency: low
828	    enabled: true
829	```
830	
831	- **Job runner** uses APScheduler (in-process, cron syntax). Wakes when due, invokes the skill with the persona, enqueues the result with the configured urgency. Same delivery worker, same `before_send` handlers.
832	
833	- **Urgency rules** in `config/urgency.yaml`:
834	
835	```yaml
836	rules:
837	  - source: calendar
838	    when: starts_within_minutes <= 15
839	    urgency: urgent
840	  - source: email
841	    when: from_in: ["dalibor@kiwi.com"]
842	    urgency: normal
843	  - source: news
844	    urgency: low
845	```
846	
847	- **Job management:** `/jobs`, `/job enable <name>`, `/job disable <name>`, `/job run <name>`.
848	
849	- **Additional integrations as separate skills:** `email-triage`, `news-digest`, `reddit-research`. Each is a skill folder. Each backing capability is a CLI script the agent invokes via `bash`, not a first-class tool.
850	
851	The reason this is v0.3 and not v0.1: the queue, policy engine, events, and progressive disclosure from v0.1 mean the scheduler is mostly "enqueue stuff on a cron." The hard parts are already solved.
852	
853	---
854	
855	## Setup Instructions (for the README)
856	
857	```bash
858	# prerequisites
859	# - Python 3.11+
860	# - uv (https://docs.astral.sh/uv/)
861	# - A Telegram bot token from @BotFather
862	# - An OpenRouter API key (https://openrouter.ai/)
863	
864	git clone <repo>
865	cd ubongo
866	uv sync
867	
868	# configure
869	cp .env.example .env
870	# edit .env with your tokens
871	# edit config/settings.yaml: set telegram.allowed_user_ids
872	# (find your numeric Telegram ID by messaging @userinfobot)
873	# edit config/UBONGO.md if you want to customize identity/preferences
874	# edit config/personas/*.md to tune voices
875	
876	# run
877	uv run python -m ubongo
878	```
879	
880	---
881	
882	## Final Notes for Claude Code
883	
884	- Build phase by phase. Do not start Phase N+1 before Phase N is acceptance-tested. Use the acceptance criteria literally.
885	- Keep modules small. If a file exceeds 300 lines, consider splitting.
886	- Write tests for the queue, policy engine, compaction function, event dispatcher, and progressive-disclosure skill registry. The rest can rely on manual testing in v0.1.
887	- No new dependencies beyond those listed in the tech stack table without justification.
888	- The user's preferences apply to bot output too: prose over bullets, no em-dashes, no emojis, direct tone. These live in `config/UBONGO.md` and are inherited by every persona.
889	- Skills are loaded by description only at startup; bodies load on activation. Verify this before claiming Phase 6 complete.
890	- New behavior in v0.2+ ships as event handlers, not core-loop edits.
891	- New capabilities in v0.2+ default to CLI scripts invoked via `bash`, not first-class tools.
892	- When in doubt, defer. Anything not explicitly required for v0.1 acceptance criteria is out of scope for v0.1.
